pages.raw_text:
WASHINGTON, May lis
Roosevelt's policy of conservation
to be thrown to the dogs of monop-
oily? Are the ungrabbed natural re-
sources of the nation to be handed
over to the moneyed looters to be
exploited against the American peO-
pie during all future time?


| fear so, but took my fears to
Secretary r. A. Ballinger of the
interior department, haping that he
would explain them away.


Ballinger is the man for whom J.
A. Garfield was displaced in Taft s
cabinet. He is charged with that
great task which was so well begun
by the sturdy veteran Ethan Allen
Hitchcock. He is the man on whom
the public relies to hold in check
the predatory interests-the thiev-
ing railroads and the grabbing cor
porations. He is chief custodian of
the public lands. put my question
squarely:


"Mr. Secretary, it is said that
there has been complete reversal
of your predecessor policies. The
numerous reservations to the public
domain of land previously with
drawn appears to support that no.
ton. l would be glad to know au-
thoritatively what are the present
policies"


The secretary said he did not
wish to talk for publication, and
that he would not consent to be
quoted. This he later modified, per-
hitting this much to quotation
marks:


ut is not true that | am not for
conservation policies. Conservation
is regarded by this Office as of the
highest moment. Everything is be-
ing done looking to a proper con-
servation of the public wealth"


i called the attention of Secre-
tary Ballinger to the fact that there
were almost daily restorations of
lands which had been withdraw,
for power site examination. Also
that 100000 acres of Ignite land
in North Dakota were thrown open
to entry. Also that lands in Wyo-
ming and Idaho supposed to con-
tain phosphate had been restored.
And finally that lands withdrawn
for future reclamation works were
being sent back again to the public
domain.


The secretary then explained
that he had given orders for a
general examination of lands suit
able for power site purposes and
valuable for other reasons, and
had recommended that congress
pass some law for their future dis
position
